fr:mainmatter.html:p: Suppose we have introduced an implication only to immediately eliminate it. We want to make sure that this process doesn't smuggle any new information in; we should only be able to get out of an implication what we put in. When we introduce an implication and immediately eliminate it, here's what we end up looking at:

Recall that this doesn't mean that any assumptions of A must be discharged. But it does mean that some of them might be. In any particular case, of course, we will be able to tell whether or not any have. Let's look at a couple of examples.
In this first example, we will consider a case where no discharge actually happens:

In this proof we have a local peak at (q \lor r) \rightarrow p, and this local peak is introduced by a step of \rightarrow I that does not discharge any open assumptions. The overall proof is a proof of any argument with the conclusion p and with p \land q and r among its premises. Can you spot a way to use pieces of this proof to get a proof of any such argument, without going through the local peak?
Here's one:

This is just the top left bit of the original proof; it already did everything we need! As in the case of conjunction, this smaller proof is a proof of even more arguments than the original, since there is no longer any need to have r among the argument's premises; but this is still no problem. The smaller proof proves all the arguments that the original one does.

Let's generalise this to see how it works more generally, but still just for those cases where the step of \rightarrow I introducing the local peak doesn't discharge anything. In these cases, we're looking at something like this:
And we can simplify this directly to:

That is, however the B in the original proof is proved, we can just keep that part of the original proof and throw the rest away. The resulting proof has conclusion B, just like the original proof, and its open assumptions, whatever they are, must be a subset of the original proof's open assumptions. So whenever the larger proof is a proof of any argument, the smaller proof is a proof of that argument as well.
That takes care of cases where the \rightarrow I that introduces the local peak doesn't discharge anything. But what about when it does? Here we have more work to do. Let's look at an example:

This is a proof of any argument with q as a conclusion and p \rightarrow (r \rightarrow q), p, r among its premises. But if we try clipping out just the bit above the local peak on the left, like we did in the cases without discharge, we end up here:

Call that piece L (for left). Since we've removed the local peak, we don't have the discharging rule \rightarrow I in place anymore to discharge the assumptions of p \land r, so these are open assumptions in piece L, even though they were not open the larger one. As a result, there are arguments, such as p \rightarrow (r \rightarrow q), p, r \;/\; q, that are proved by the larger proof but not by piece L. So there's an issue to solve.
The solution is to note that, in the larger proof, there was another bit of it feeding into the \rightarrow E, besides the introduction that created the local peak:

Call that piece R (for right). This is a proof of our 'extra' assumption p \land r, and its open assumptions in turn were open in the original proof. So we can plug piece R in at the originally-discharged assumptions of p \land r that are sitting open in piece L. This gets us to:

Make sure you see how this is built by combining piece L and piece R from the original proof: we have lifted piece L out, noted that it has extra open assumptions, and then plugged in a copy of piece R at each of these. The result in this case is a proof of q with open assumptions p \rightarrow (r \rightarrow q), p, r, the same as our original proof. But we no longer have the local peak at (p \land r) \rightarrow q that the original proof had.

(There are, however, two new local peaks in this proof, both of the sentence p \land r. This is a sign that removing all local peaks from a whole proof might be a complex affair, since the process of removing one can create others. We won't look deeper into that this semester, but it's really at the heart of deep study of natural deduction systems.)
Once again, let's see if we can generalise this. When we have a local peak using implication, it looks like:

Here the two dotted-line parts correspond to piece L and piece R. Since the indicated step of \rightarrow I can have discharged assumptions of A, piece L might have A as an open assumption even though the whole proof does not; that's the issue we're working on handling.
We can reduce the above to this result:

This is just piece R plugged in above piece L, whatever those pieces were in the original proof. As we saw in the above example, this might indicate multiple copies of piece R, since we need one copy of piece R for every open assumption discharged by the \rightarrow I that created the local peak.
Recall that we only have a local peak when the implication introduced is the leftmost input to the implication elimination. What about a proof that looks like this?

Here there's nothing to do; this is just not a local peak. The implication that we're eliminating here is (A \rightarrow B) \rightarrow C; that is, this is the sentence whose main connective is the one that needs to link up with the elimination rule we're using. This is one reason we always care about the order of the inputs into rules like \rightarrow E: it makes it easy to see this distinction at a glance, and lets us just say 'leftmost' in defining local peaks.

But what is a 'connective like tonk'? Here there is substantial disagreement; I'll just explain one line of thought that's proved influential. On this line of thought, the trouble with tonk is that by introducing it and then immediately eliminating it, we can get somewhere that we couldn't get otherwise. Consider, for example, the following proof using tonk:

In NJ, there is no proof at all of p \lor q \;/\; r \land s, no proof of r \land s without some open assumptions other than p \lor q. But once we have tonk in the mix, we get the above proof of this argument. On the way of thinking I'm presenting, this is a problem with tonk.

Our connectives, we might think, should be ways of packaging up and unpackaging pieces of information in various forms. But if that's so, we shouldn't be able to pull something out of a package that we didn't put in it in the first place!

So on this way of thinking, what we need for each connective's rules is that, whenever we introduce a connective and then immediately eliminate that same connective, we don't get anywhere that we couldn't have already gotten without doing that. Showing that this is the case is what reduction is all about.
In the above proof, we introduce and then immediately eliminate tonk. The sentence that bridges the introduction and elimination---in that example, (p \lor q) \# (r \land s)---is called a local peak.
An occurrence of a sentence is a local peak in a proof iff: it is at the same time the conclusion of an introduction rule and also the leftmost premise of an elimination rule.
(Note the restriction to 'leftmost' there; we'll talk about it soon.)

Reduction is then the process of flattening local peaks in a proof: finding a way to get a proof that works without the local peak to achieve the same result. As we've seen above, this is not always possible once tonk is in our language. But we can do it with all our more familiar connectives. Let's look connective by connective at how this works.

Suppose we have a local peak for conjunction. Since conjunction has one introduction rule and two elimination rules, there are two possible ways this could arise. The general forms of these two ways are:

Now, think for a moment: if you come across a proof with the shape on the left, what do you have? You have a proof of A with the open assumptions A, B. That suffices to be a proof of any argument \Gamma , A, B \;/\; A. Could you provide a proof of that same argument that does not have this local peak?

Totally! Just a single assumption of A would do. And similarly, for the form on the right, any argument that this form is a proof of also has as a proof just a single assumption of B. So when it comes to our conjunction rules, these do allow us to flatten any local peaks.
There's an important move I just made in the above reasoning, and I want to linger on it a bit here before we go on to other connectives. Consider the following instance of the left-hand form above:
As we've seen, I've suggested that we can do the same work just by assuming p \lor q. But what is 'the same work'? There are a number of ways to understand this, some more complex and precise.

But for our purposes, we can use a simple answer: for any argument at all that the proof with a local peak is a proof of, the simple assumption of p \lor q is also a proof of that same argument. After all, if the proof with the local peak is a proof of an argument, that argument must have the conclusion p \lor q, and it must have p \lor q and r among its premises. So the assumption of p \lor q, which is a proof with conclusion p \lor q and the lone open assumption p \lor q, is also a proof of that argument, whatever it is.

Note that there are some arguments, like for example p \lor q, r \land s \;/\; p \lor q, where the above proof with a local peak is not a proof of the argument but the simple assumption of p \lor q is. one. That's fine: the reduced proof, in this case the assumption of p \lor q, can be a proof of more arguments than the original. We just need to find a way to get rid of the local peak without losing any arguments.

Suppose we have introduced an implication only to immediately eliminate it. We want to make sure that this process doesn't smuggle any new information in; we should only be able to get out of an implication what we put in. When we introduce an implication and immediately eliminate it, here's what we end up looking at:

Recall that this doesn't mean that any assumptions of A must be discharged. But it does mean that some of them might be. In any particular case, of course, we will be able to tell whether or not any have. Let's look at a couple of examples.
In this first example, we will consider a case where no discharge actually happens:

In this proof we have a local peak at (q \lor r) \rightarrow p, and this local peak is introduced by a step of \rightarrow I that does not discharge any open assumptions. The overall proof is a proof of any argument with the conclusion p and with p \land q and r among its premises. Can you spot a way to use pieces of this proof to get a proof of any such argument, without going through the local peak?
Here's one:

This is just the top left bit of the original proof; it already did everything we need! As in the case of conjunction, this smaller proof is a proof of even more arguments than the original, since there is no longer any need to have r among the argument's premises; but this is still no problem. The smaller proof proves all the arguments that the original one does.

Let's generalise this to see how it works more generally, but still just for those cases where the step of \rightarrow I introducing the local peak doesn't discharge anything. In these cases, we're looking at something like this:
And we can simplify this directly to:

That is, however the B in the original proof is proved, we can just keep that part of the original proof and throw the rest away. The resulting proof has conclusion B, just like the original proof, and its open assumptions, whatever they are, must be a subset of the original proof's open assumptions. So whenever the larger proof is a proof of any argument, the smaller proof is a proof of that argument as well.
That takes care of cases where the \rightarrow I that introduces the local peak doesn't discharge anything. But what about when it does? Here we have more work to do. Let's look at an example:

This is a proof of any argument with q as a conclusion and p \rightarrow (r \rightarrow q), p, r among its premises. But if we try clipping out just the bit above the local peak on the left, like we did in the cases without discharge, we end up here:

Call that piece L (for left). Since we've removed the local peak, we don't have the discharging rule \rightarrow I in place anymore to discharge the assumptions of p \land r, so these are open assumptions in piece L, even though they were not open the larger one. As a result, there are arguments, such as p \rightarrow (r \rightarrow q), p, r \;/\; q, that are proved by the larger proof but not by piece L. So there's an issue to solve.
The solution is to note that, in the larger proof, there was another bit of it feeding into the \rightarrow E, besides the introduction that created the local peak:

Call that piece R (for right). This is a proof of our 'extra' assumption p \land r, and its open assumptions in turn were open in the original proof. So we can plug piece R in at the originally-discharged assumptions of p \land r that are sitting open in piece L. This gets us to:

Make sure you see how this is built by combining piece L and piece R from the original proof: we have lifted piece L out, noted that it has extra open assumptions, and then plugged in a copy of piece R at each of these. The result in this case is a proof of q with open assumptions p \rightarrow (r \rightarrow q), p, r, the same as our original proof. But we no longer have the local peak at (p \land r) \rightarrow q that the original proof had.

(There are, however, two new local peaks in this proof, both of the sentence p \land r. This is a sign that removing all local peaks from a whole proof might be a complex affair, since the process of removing one can create others. We won't look deeper into that this semester, but it's really at the heart of deep study of natural deduction systems.)
Once again, let's see if we can generalise this. When we have a local peak using implication, it looks like:

Here the two dotted-line parts correspond to piece L and piece R. Since the indicated step of \rightarrow I can have discharged assumptions of A, piece L might have A as an open assumption even though the whole proof does not; that's the issue we're working on handling.
We can reduce the above to this result:

This is just piece R plugged in above piece L, whatever those pieces were in the original proof. As we saw in the above example, this might indicate multiple copies of piece R, since we need one copy of piece R for every open assumption discharged by the \rightarrow I that created the local peak.
Recall that we only have a local peak when the implication introduced is the leftmost input to the implication elimination. What about a proof that looks like this?

Here there's nothing to do; this is just not a local peak. The implication that we're eliminating here is (A \rightarrow B) \rightarrow C; that is, this is the sentence whose main connective is the one that needs to link up with the elimination rule we're using. This is one reason we always care about the order of the inputs into rules like \rightarrow E: it makes it easy to see this distinction at a glance, and lets us just say 'leftmost' in defining local peaks.

Now that you've seen how it works for conjunction and implication, you're all set for disjunction; nothing new here. Since disjunction has two introduction rules and one elimination rule, a local peak can arise in one of two ways. Let's look at \lor I_l first:
Here, we can just take the A that comes into the \lor I_l and plug it for any discharged assumptions of A in the middle subproof, getting to:

This is exactly as in the case of implication, except that here we had to make sure to take the middle subproof, since that's the one that might discharge A, which is what we have on the left. What about when the local peak is created from \lor I_r instead? Like this:
Here, we would want to take the rightmost subproof instead of the middle one, since what we have on the left is a B, and that's what might be discharged on the right. So we would arrive at:

In both of these reduced proofs, just as with implication, we would need to copy over the left A or B, whichever one we have, as many times as there are assumptions discharged in the subproof we're selecting.
Reduction for negation is even more like implication than disjunction is. When we have a local peak for negation, we have:
Just like for implication, we take the right input to the elimination rule and plug it in for any assumptions of A discharged in the left input, arriving at:
Remember that the idea of reduction steps is to show that we can flatten all local peaks that can be produced by the introduction and elimination rules for a connective.

When it comes to verum and falsum, however, there can never be any local peaks: verum has no elimination rules and falsum has no introduction rules! So there is nothing to worry about: we have succeeded at flattening all of the local peaks these connectives can produce, since there are no such local peaks.
